title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: The Art of Movement: Parkour and Freerunning
content.text: A young man runs along a rooftop, springs off a low wall, rolls smoothly onto the pavement below and launches himself over a railing without breaking stride. To a bystander it looks like an act of recklessness. To a practitioner of parkour, it is a precisely calculated sequence of movements, years of training condensed into a few fluid seconds. (37) [37] Parkour teaches its practitioners to see urban environments not as obstacles but as opportunities for creative movement. [/37] || The discipline was developed in France in the 1980s and 1990s by David Belle, drawing on the military obstacle-course training his father had practised. Belle and a group of friends — the Yamakasi — refined these movements into a philosophy of efficient, direct passage through any environment. (38) [38] The core principle was that a practitioner should always choose the most direct and efficient route, regardless of what lay in the way. [/38] || Freerunning is a related but distinct discipline. Where parkour emphasises efficiency and directness, freerunning prioritises creative expression — flips, spins and acrobatic movements that may be visually spectacular but are not necessarily the fastest way from A to B. (39) [39] Both, however, demand the same foundation of strength, body awareness and spatial reasoning. [/39] || Training is a long, methodical process. Beginners start with basic movements close to the ground — precision jumps, controlled landings, and falling safely. (40) [40] The community places enormous emphasis on not attempting moves beyond one's current ability, which runs counter to the daredevil image that media coverage often projects. [/40] || Parkour has attracted growing academic interest. Sports scientists study its demands on the musculoskeletal system; psychologists examine how practitioners assess and manage risk. (41) [41] The emerging picture is of a discipline that develops not only physical fitness but also calm decision-making under pressure. [/41] || Many cities now offer legitimate parkour classes and designated training areas. (42) [42] As parkour moves into gyms, schools and community centres, it is gradually shedding its outlaw image and finding its place alongside other recognised movement arts. [/42]

paragraphs.A: The discipline has even begun to appear in physical education curricula in some countries.
paragraphs.B: Progress is gradual and deliberate, with each new move built on a solid foundation of previously mastered skills.
paragraphs.C: The two disciplines share an origin but have since diverged into distinct competitive scenes.
paragraphs.D: This philosophy was as much mental as physical: the goal was to overcome fear and hesitation as much as physical barriers.
paragraphs.E: What began as a small subcultural movement has since attracted millions of practitioners worldwide.
paragraphs.F: The mental resilience developed through training transfers surprisingly well to everyday stressful situations.
paragraphs.G: For many practitioners, this is where the true challenge lies — not in the physical movements themselves, but in the mental approach to the environment.

questions.explanation: G explains that the real challenge is the mental perspective towards the environment, complementing the introduction about seeing the city as an opportunity for movement.
D describes that the philosophy was both mental and physical and that the goal was to overcome fear, supporting the principle of the most direct and efficient route.
C notes that both disciplines share an origin but have diverged into distinct competitive scenes, connecting the differences between parkour and freerunning.
B describes the gradual and deliberate progress built on previously mastered skills, supporting the methodical training process.
F indicates that the mental resilience developed in training transfers to stressful everyday situations, supporting the benefits beyond the physical.
A mentions that parkour has begun to appear in physical education curricula, supporting the transition towards recognised and legitimised spaces.
